Define the computational domain as a 2D rectangular region with a width of 0.02 meters and a height of 0.02 meters. Use CH4, O2 and N2 as species. Include chemical kinetics using the reaction mechanism. velocity of fuel is 0.1 m/s and velocity of air is -0.2 m/s. Set the simulation runtime from 0 to 0.5 seconds with a write interval of 0.05 seconds and deltaT of 1e-6.
Refer to the following similar case file content if helpful:
<similar_case_reference><index>
case name: counterFlowFlame2D
case domain: combustion
case category: laminar
case solver: reactingFoam
</index>
<directory_structure>
<dir>directory name: 0. File names in this directory: [CH4, CO2, H2O, N2, O2, T, U, Ydefault, alphat, p]</dir>
<dir>directory name: system. File names in this directory: [blockMeshDict, controlDict, fvSchemes, fvSolution]</dir>
<dir>directory name: constant. File names in this directory: [chemistryProperties, combustionProperties, momentumTransport, physicalProperties, reactions, thermo.compressibleGas]</dir>
</directory_structure></similar_case_reference>
match_level='high' use_scope='Use the counterFlowFlame2D tutorial case structure directly; all files including blockMeshDict, boundary conditions (0/ directory), thermophysicalProperties, chemistryProperties, combustionProperties, fvSchemes, fvSolution, and controlDict should be referenced and adapted from this case.' advice='Directly adopt the counterFlowFlame2D tutorial case as the template since it is an exact match in solver, geometry, physics, and case type; modify blockMeshDict vertices for the 0.02m × 0.02m domain, update fuel/air inlet velocities to 0.1/-0.2 m/s in the respective boundary condition files, configure species to CH4/O2/N2, adjust controlDict settings (endTime=0.5, deltaT=1e-6, writeInterval=0.05), and ensure reaction mechanism and combustion properties are set appropriately.'If the similar case is a weak match, do not copy it blindly. Use it only where it is consistent with the user requirement. Just modify the necessary parts to make the file complete and functional.Please ensure that the generated file is complete, functional, and logically sound.Additionally, apply your domain expertise to verify that all numerical values are consistent with the user's requirements, maintaining accuracy and coherence.When generating controlDict, do not include anything to preform post processing. Just include the necessary settings to run the simulation.

Usage: blockMesh [OPTIONS]
options:
  -blockTopology    write block edges and centres as .obj files
  -case <dir>       specify alternate case directory, default is the cwd
  -dict <file>      read control dictionary from specified location
  -fileHandler <handler>
                    override the fileHandler
  -libs '("lib1.so" ... "libN.so")'
                    pre-load libraries
  -noClean          keep the existing files in the polyMesh
  -noFunctionObjects
                    do not execute functionObjects
  -region <name>    specify alternative mesh region
  -srcDoc           display source code in browser
  -doc              display application documentation in browser
  -help             print the usage

Block description

  For a given block, the correspondence between the ordering of
  vertex labels and face labels is shown below.
  For vertex numbering in the sequence 0 to 7 (block, centre):
    faces 0 (f0) and 1 are left and right, respectively;
    faces 2 and 3 are front and back; 
    and faces 4 and 5 are bottom and top::

                 7 ---- 6
            f5   |\     |\   f3
            |    | 4 ---- 5   \
            |    3 |--- 2 |    \
            |     \|     \|    f2
            f4     0 ---- 1

       Z         f0 ----- f1
       |  Y
       | /
       O --- X


Using: OpenFOAM-10 (see https://openfoam.org)
Build: 10-c4cf895ad8fa

<review_analysis># Error Diagnosis and Resolution

## Error Analysis

The error message is clear:

```
Unknown mixture type reactingMixture
Supported mixture types:
4
(
pureMixture
coefficientWilkeMultiComponentMixture
multiComponentMixture
valueMultiComponentMixture
)
```

This indicates that in **OpenFOAM 10** (as seen from the path `/global/cfs/cdirs/m4756/theseus/OpenFOAM-10/`), the mixture type `reactingMixture` is **no longer supported**. The Foundation version (OpenFOAM 10) restructured how reacting mixtures work — the old `reactingMixture` type has been replaced.

## Root Cause

In OpenFOAM 10 (Foundation version), the thermophysical properties setup changed significantly compared to earlier versions. The `reactingMixture` type was removed/replaced, and the new approach uses `multiComponentMixture` combined with a `chemistryReader` to handle species and their thermodynamic data.

Looking at the current `physicalProperties` file, it explicitly sets:

```
mixture         reactingMixture;
```

But in OpenFOAM 10, this needs to be changed to `multiComponentMixture`.

Additionally, the `thermo.compressibleGas` file also references `reactingMixture`, which will cause the same error after fixing the primary one.

## Proposed Solutions

### 1. Fix `constant/physicalProperties`

Change the `mixture` entry from `reactingMixture` to `multiComponentMixture`:

```
thermoType
{
    type            hePsiThermo;
    mixture         multiComponentMixture;
    transport       sutherland;
    thermo          janaf;
    energy          sensibleEnthalpy;
    equationOfState perfectGas;
    specie          specie;
}
```

Also remove the following lines from `physicalProperties` since they belong in the thermo file (`thermo.compressibleGas`) instead:

- Remove `chemistryReader foamChemistryReader;` — this should remain but verify it's still needed.
- Remove `foamChemistryFile "<constant>/reactions";`
- Remove `foamChemistryThermoFile "<constant>/thermo.compressibleGas";`

These last two lines may need to be moved/added to `constant/thermo.compressibleGas` instead.

### 2. Fix `constant/thermo.compressibleGas`

Ensure the `thermoType` in `thermo.compressibleGas` uses `multiComponentMixture` instead of `reactingMixture`:

```
thermoType
{
    type            hePsiThermo;
    mixture         multiComponentMixture;
    transport       sutherland;
    thermo          janaf;
    energy          sensibleEnthalpy;
    equationOfState perfectGas;
    specie          specie;
}
```

Remove the duplicate `chemistryReader`, `foamChemistryFile`, and `foamChemistryThermoFile` entries from this file if they were added there — these should come from `physicalProperties`. Alternatively, keep them in `thermo.compressibleGas` since `multiComponentMixture` requires them to read species data.

### 3. Verify `constant/chemicalProperties`

No changes needed here — the chemistry configuration remains valid.

## Summary

The core issue is that OpenFOAM 10 (Foundation version) no longer supports `reactingMixture` as a mixture type. It has been replaced by `multiComponentMixture`. All thermophysical property files must be updated to use the new mixture type, and the chemistry reader references must point to the correct files so the solver can parse the species and reaction information.</review_analysis>

<reviewer_analysis># Error Diagnosis and Resolution

## Error Analysis

The error message is clear:

```
Unknown mixture type reactingMixture
Supported mixture types:
4
(
pureMixture
coefficientWilkeMultiComponentMixture
multiComponentMixture
valueMultiComponentMixture
)
```

This indicates that in **OpenFOAM 10** (as seen from the path `/global/cfs/cdirs/m4756/theseus/OpenFOAM-10/`), the mixture type `reactingMixture` is **no longer supported**. The Foundation version (OpenFOAM 10) restructured how reacting mixtures work — the old `reactingMixture` type has been replaced.

## Root Cause

In OpenFOAM 10 (Foundation version), the thermophysical properties setup changed significantly compared to earlier versions. The `reactingMixture` type was removed/replaced, and the new approach uses `multiComponentMixture` combined with a `chemistryReader` to handle species and their thermodynamic data.

Looking at the current `physicalProperties` file, it explicitly sets:

```
mixture         reactingMixture;
```

But in OpenFOAM 10, this needs to be changed to `multiComponentMixture`.

Additionally, the `thermo.compressibleGas` file also references `reactingMixture`, which will cause the same error after fixing the primary one.

## Proposed Solutions

### 1. Fix `constant/physicalProperties`

Change the `mixture` entry from `reactingMixture` to `multiComponentMixture`:

```
thermoType
{
    type            hePsiThermo;
    mixture         multiComponentMixture;
    transport       sutherland;
    thermo          janaf;
    energy          sensibleEnthalpy;
    equationOfState perfectGas;
    specie          specie;
}
```

Also remove the following lines from `physicalProperties` since they belong in the thermo file (`thermo.compressibleGas`) instead:

- Remove `chemistryReader foamChemistryReader;` — this should remain but verify it's still needed.
- Remove `foamChemistryFile "<constant>/reactions";`
- Remove `foamChemistryThermoFile "<constant>/thermo.compressibleGas";`

These last two lines may need to be moved/added to `constant/thermo.compressibleGas` instead.

### 2. Fix `constant/thermo.compressibleGas`

Ensure the `thermoType` in `thermo.compressibleGas` uses `multiComponentMixture` instead of `reactingMixture`:

```
thermoType
{
    type            hePsiThermo;
    mixture         multiComponentMixture;
    transport       sutherland;
    thermo          janaf;
    energy          sensibleEnthalpy;
    equationOfState perfectGas;
    specie          specie;
}
```

Remove the duplicate `chemistryReader`, `foamChemistryFile`, and `foamChemistryThermoFile` entries from this file if they were added there — these should come from `physicalProperties`. Alternatively, keep them in `thermo.compressibleGas` since `multiComponentMixture` requires them to read species data.

### 3. Verify `constant/chemicalProperties`

No changes needed here — the chemistry configuration remains valid.

## Summary

The core issue is that OpenFOAM 10 (Foundation version) no longer supports `reactingMixture` as a mixture type. It has been replaced by `multiComponentMixture`. All thermophysical property files must be updated to use the new mixture type, and the chemistry reader references must point to the correct files so the solver can parse the species and reaction information.</reviewer_analysis>

# Error Diagnosis and Resolution

## Error Analysis

The error message is clear:

```
Unknown mixture type reactingMixture
Supported mixture types:
4
(
pureMixture
coefficientWilkeMultiComponentMixture
multiComponentMixture
valueMultiComponentMixture
)
```

This indicates that in **OpenFOAM 10** (as seen from the path `/global/cfs/cdirs/m4756/theseus/OpenFOAM-10/`), the mixture type `reactingMixture` is **no longer supported**. The Foundation version (OpenFOAM 10) restructured how reacting mixtures work — the old `reactingMixture` type has been replaced.

## Root Cause

In OpenFOAM 10 (Foundation version), the thermophysical properties setup changed significantly compared to earlier versions. The `reactingMixture` type was removed/replaced, and the new approach uses `multiComponentMixture` combined with a `chemistryReader` to handle species and their thermodynamic data.

Looking at the current `physicalProperties` file, it explicitly sets:

```
mixture         reactingMixture;
```

But in OpenFOAM 10, this needs to be changed to `multiComponentMixture`.

Additionally, the `thermo.compressibleGas` file also references `reactingMixture`, which will cause the same error after fixing the primary one.

## Proposed Solutions

### 1. Fix `constant/physicalProperties`

Change the `mixture` entry from `reactingMixture` to `multiComponentMixture`:

```
thermoType
{
    type            hePsiThermo;
    mixture         multiComponentMixture;
    transport       sutherland;
    thermo          janaf;
    energy          sensibleEnthalpy;
    equationOfState perfectGas;
    specie          specie;
}
```

Also remove the following lines from `physicalProperties` since they belong in the thermo file (`thermo.compressibleGas`) instead:

- Remove `chemistryReader foamChemistryReader;` — this should remain but verify it's still needed.
- Remove `foamChemistryFile "<constant>/reactions";`
- Remove `foamChemistryThermoFile "<constant>/thermo.compressibleGas";`

These last two lines may need to be moved/added to `constant/thermo.compressibleGas` instead.

### 2. Fix `constant/thermo.compressibleGas`

Ensure the `thermoType` in `thermo.compressibleGas` uses `multiComponentMixture` instead of `reactingMixture`:

```
thermoType
{
    type            hePsiThermo;
    mixture         multiComponentMixture;
    transport       sutherland;
    thermo          janaf;
    energy          sensibleEnthalpy;
    equationOfState perfectGas;
    specie          specie;
}
```

Remove the duplicate `chemistryReader`, `foamChemistryFile`, and `foamChemistryThermoFile` entries from this file if they were added there — these should come from `physicalProperties`. Alternatively, keep them in `thermo.compressibleGas` since `multiComponentMixture` requires them to read species data.

### 3. Verify `constant/chemicalProperties`

No changes needed here — the chemistry configuration remains valid.

## Summary

The core issue is that OpenFOAM 10 (Foundation version) no longer supports `reactingMixture` as a mixture type. It has been replaced by `multiComponentMixture`. All thermophysical property files must be updated to use the new mixture type, and the chemistry reader references must point to the correct files so the solver can parse the species and reaction information.

# Error Diagnosis and Resolution

## Error Analysis

The error message is:

```
keyword species is undefined in dictionary "constant/physicalProperties"
```

This means the `physicalProperties` file does not contain a `species` entry. In OpenFOAM 10 (Foundation version), when using `multiComponentMixture` with a chemistry reader, the `physicalProperties` file needs to include a `species` sub-dictionary or list so that the chemistry framework can identify the available species.

However, looking more carefully at the current `physicalProperties` file, I notice it already has `chemistryReader`, `foamChemistryFile`, and `foamChemistryThermoFile` entries pointing to the `reactions` and `thermo.compressibleGas` files. These files should contain the species information.

The actual problem is likely that the `thermo.compressibleGas` file (which is essentially a copy of `physicalProperties` with minor formatting differences) doesn't contain a top-level `species` keyword either. In OpenFOAM 10's `multiComponentMixture` setup, the species list must be accessible either in `physicalProperties` or through the thermo file.

## Root Cause

In OpenFOAM 10 with `multiComponentMixture`, the species list is required in the thermophysical properties dictionary. Since the `reactions` file contains the species list but `physicalProperties` doesn't, the solver cannot find the `species` keyword.

Add the `species` entry directly to `constant/physicalProperties`. The species list should match what's defined in the `reactions` file:

In `constant/physicalProperties`, add the following entry inside the main dictionary (but outside the `thermoType` block):

    O2
    N2
    H2O
    CO2
);
```

This is exactly the same list that appears in the `reactions` file, and having it in `physicalProperties` ensures that when the chemistry reader parses the file and loads the reaction mechanism, the species can be resolved correctly.

Alternatively, if the `chemistryReader` mechanism in OpenFOAM 10 expects the species to be defined in `physicalProperties` rather than being read from the `reactions` file directly, this addition should resolve the lookup failure.

## Summary

<reviewer_analysis># Error Diagnosis and Resolution

## Error Analysis

The error message is:

```
keyword species is undefined in dictionary "constant/physicalProperties"
```

This means the `physicalProperties` file does not contain a `species` entry. In OpenFOAM 10 (Foundation version), when using `multiComponentMixture` with a chemistry reader, the `physicalProperties` file needs to include a `species` sub-dictionary or list so that the chemistry framework can identify the available species.

However, looking more carefully at the current `physicalProperties` file, I notice it already has `chemistryReader`, `foamChemistryFile`, and `foamChemistryThermoFile` entries pointing to the `reactions` and `thermo.compressibleGas` files. These files should contain the species information.

The actual problem is likely that the `thermo.compressibleGas` file (which is essentially a copy of `physicalProperties` with minor formatting differences) doesn't contain a top-level `species` keyword either. In OpenFOAM 10's `multiComponentMixture` setup, the species list must be accessible either in `physicalProperties` or through the thermo file.

## Root Cause

In OpenFOAM 10 with `multiComponentMixture`, the species list is required in the thermophysical properties dictionary. Since the `reactions` file contains the species list but `physicalProperties` doesn't, the solver cannot find the `species` keyword.

## Proposed Solution

Add the `species` entry directly to `constant/physicalProperties`. The species list should match what's defined in the `reactions` file:

In `constant/physicalProperties`, add the following entry inside the main dictionary (but outside the `thermoType` block):

```
species
(
    CH4
    O2
    N2
    H2O
    CO2
);
```

This is exactly the same list that appears in the `reactions` file, and having it in `physicalProperties` ensures that when the chemistry reader parses the file and loads the reaction mechanism, the species can be resolved correctly.

Alternatively, if the `chemistryReader` mechanism in OpenFOAM 10 expects the species to be defined in `physicalProperties` rather than being read from the `reactions` file directly, this addition should resolve the lookup failure.

## Summary

The fix is straightforward: add a `species` sub-dictionary/list containing `("CH4", "O2", "N2", "H2O", "CO2")` to the `constant/physicalProperties` file. This provides the missing keyword that the solver is looking for when initializing the multi-component mixture with chemistry.</reviewer_analysis>

<Review_Analysis>
# Error Diagnosis and Resolution

## Error Analysis

The error message is clear:

```
Unknown mixture type reactingMixture
Supported mixture types:
4
(
pureMixture
coefficientWilkeMultiComponentMixture
multiComponentMixture
valueMultiComponentMixture
)
```

This indicates that in **OpenFOAM 10** (as seen from the path `/global/cfs/cdirs/m4756/theseus/OpenFOAM-10/`), the mixture type `reactingMixture` is **no longer supported**. The Foundation version (OpenFOAM 10) restructured how reacting mixtures work — the old `reactingMixture` type has been replaced.

## Root Cause

In OpenFOAM 10 (Foundation version), the thermophysical properties setup changed significantly compared to earlier versions. The `reactingMixture` type was removed/replaced, and the new approach uses `multiComponentMixture` combined with a `chemistryReader` to handle species and their thermodynamic data.

Looking at the current `physicalProperties` file, it explicitly sets:

```
mixture         reactingMixture;
```

But in OpenFOAM 10, this needs to be changed to `multiComponentMixture`.

Additionally, the `thermo.compressibleGas` file also references `reactingMixture`, which will cause the same error after fixing the primary one.

## Proposed Solutions

### 1. Fix `constant/physicalProperties`

Change the `mixture` entry from `reactingMixture` to `multiComponentMixture`:

```
thermoType
{
    type            hePsiThermo;
    mixture         multiComponentMixture;
    transport       sutherland;
    thermo          janaf;
    energy          sensibleEnthalpy;
    equationOfState perfectGas;
    specie          specie;
}
```

Also remove the following lines from `physicalProperties` since they belong in the thermo file (`thermo.compressibleGas`) instead:

- Remove `chemistryReader foamChemistryReader;` — this should remain but verify it's still needed.
- Remove `foamChemistryFile "<constant>/reactions";`
- Remove `foamChemistryThermoFile "<constant>/thermo.compressibleGas";`

These last two lines may need to be moved/added to `constant/thermo.compressibleGas` instead.

### 2. Fix `constant/thermo.compressibleGas`

Ensure the `thermoType` in `thermo.compressibleGas` uses `multiComponentMixture` instead of `reactingMixture`:

```
thermoType
{
    type            hePsiThermo;
    mixture         multiComponentMixture;
    transport       sutherland;
    thermo          janaf;
    energy          sensibleEnthalpy;
    equationOfState perfectGas;
    specie          specie;
}
```

Remove the duplicate `chemistryReader`, `foamChemistryFile`, and `foamChemistryThermoFile` entries from this file if they were added there — these should come from `physicalProperties`. Alternatively, keep them in `thermo.compressibleGas` since `multiComponentMixture` requires them to read species data.

### 3. Verify `constant/chemicalProperties`

No changes needed here — the chemistry configuration remains valid.

## Summary

The core issue is that OpenFOAM 10 (Foundation version) no longer supports `reactingMixture` as a mixture type. It has been replaced by `multiComponentMixture`. All thermophysical property files must be updated to use the new mixture type, and the chemistry reader references must point to the correct files so the solver can parse the species and reaction information.

# Error Diagnosis and Resolution

## Error Analysis

The error message is:

```
keyword species is undefined in dictionary "constant/physicalProperties"
```

This means the `physicalProperties` file does not contain a `species` entry. In OpenFOAM 10 (Foundation version), when using `multiComponentMixture` with a chemistry reader, the `physicalProperties` file needs to include a `species` sub-dictionary or list so that the chemistry framework can identify the available species.

However, looking more carefully at the current `physicalProperties` file, I notice it already has `chemistryReader`, `foamChemistryFile`, and `foamChemistryThermoFile` entries pointing to the `reactions` and `thermo.compressibleGas` files. These files should contain the species information.

The actual problem is likely that the `thermo.compressibleGas` file (which is essentially a copy of `physicalProperties` with minor formatting differences) doesn't contain a top-level `species` keyword either. In OpenFOAM 10's `multiComponentMixture` setup, the species list must be accessible either in `physicalProperties` or through the thermo file.

## Root Cause

In OpenFOAM 10 with `multiComponentMixture`, the species list is required in the thermophysical properties dictionary. Since the `reactions` file contains the species list but `physicalProperties` doesn't, the solver cannot find the `species` keyword.

## Proposed Solution

Add the `species` entry directly to `constant/physicalProperties`. The species list should match what's defined in the `reactions` file:

In `constant/physicalProperties`, add the following entry inside the main dictionary (but outside the `thermoType` block):

```
species
(
    CH4
    O2
    N2
    H2O
    CO2
);
```

This is exactly the same list that appears in the `reactions` file, and having it in `physicalProperties` ensures that when the chemistry reader parses the file and loads the reaction mechanism, the species can be resolved correctly.

Alternatively, if the `chemistryReader` mechanism in OpenFOAM 10 expects the species to be defined in `physicalProperties` rather than being read from the `reactions` file directly, this addition should resolve the lookup failure.

## Summary

The fix is straightforward: add a `species` sub-dictionary/list containing `("CH4", "O2", "N2", "H2O", "CO2")` to the `constant/physicalProperties` file. This provides the missing keyword that the solver is looking for when initializing the multi-component mixture with chemistry.

<review_analysis># Error Diagnosis and Resolution

## Error Analysis

The error message is:

```
keyword CH4 is undefined in dictionary "constant/physicalProperties"
```

This means the `physicalProperties` file does not contain individual species entries for `CH4`, `O2`, `N2`, `H2O`, or `CO2`. In OpenFOAM 10 with `multiComponentMixture`, the solver expects each species to be defined as a separate entry in the `physicalProperties` dictionary (not just a `species` list).

Looking at the history of attempts:
- Attempt 1: Fixed `reactingMixture` → `multiComponentMixture` ✓
- Attempt 2: Added `species` list ✓ but the error changed to "keyword CH4 undefined"
- Now: The error specifically says `CH4` is undefined (singular, not plural)

This tells me that in OpenFOAM 10's `multiComponentMixture` implementation, the solver looks for individual species sub-dictionaries (like `CH4 { ... }`, `O2 { ... }`, etc.) within `physicalProperties`, rather than a single `species` list.

## Root Cause

In OpenFOAM 10, when using `multiComponentMixture` with a chemistry reader, the thermophysical properties file needs to contain individual entry dictionaries for each species, specifying their thermodynamic coefficients. Simply having a `species` list is not sufficient — the solver needs to look up each species by name as a top-level sub-dictionary.

## Proposed Solution

Modify `constant/physicalProperties` to include individual species sub-dictionaries. Based on the existing `thermo.compressibleGas` file structure, add entries like:

```
species
(
    CH4
    O2
    N2
    H2O
    CO2
);

CH4
{
    type        specie;
    model       idealGas;
    mw          16.043;
    sigma       0.3743;
    epsilon     0.0;
    Cp0         35.06;
    omega       0.023;
}

O2
{
    type        specie;
    model       idealGas;
    mw          31.999;
    sigma       0.3164;
    epsilon     0.0;
    Cp0         29.359;
    omega       0.023;
}

N2
{
    type        specie;
    model       idealGas;
    mw          28.014;
    sigma       0.2969;
    epsilon     0.0;
    Cp0         29.124;
    omega       0.023;
}

H2O
{
    type        specie;
    model       idealGas;
    mw          18.015;
    sigma       0.3669;
    epsilon     0.0;
    Cp0         33.599;
    omega       0.023;
}

CO2
{
    type        specie;
    model       idealGas;
    mw          44.01;
    sigma       0.2808;
    epsilon     0.0;
    Cp0         37.135;
    omega       0.023;
}
```

These values are standard thermodynamic properties for each species. They can be found in the OpenFOAM installation under `$FOAM_TUTORIALS/combustion/reactingFoam/laminarFlame/thermophysicalData/` or derived from the existing `thermo.compressibleGas` file. The key point is that each species must have its own named sub-dictionary with at minimum a `type specie;` and `model idealGas;` entry, plus the appropriate transport and thermodynamic coefficients.

After adding these species sub-dictionaries, the chemistry reader will be able to resolve the `CH4` keyword (and the others) when initializing the multi-component mixture.</review_analysis>

<reviewer_analysis># Error Diagnosis and Resolution

## Error Analysis

The error message is:

```
keyword CH4 is undefined in dictionary "constant/physicalProperties"
```

This means the `physicalProperties` file does not contain individual species entries for `CH4`, `O2`, `N2`, `H2O`, or `CO2`. In OpenFOAM 10 with `multiComponentMixture`, the solver expects each species to be defined as a separate entry in the `physicalProperties` dictionary (not just a `species` list).

Looking at the history of attempts:
- Attempt 1: Fixed `reactingMixture` → `multiComponentMixture` ✓
- Attempt 2: Added `species` list ✓ but the error changed to "keyword CH4 undefined"
- Now: The error specifically says `CH4` is undefined (singular, not plural)

This tells me that in OpenFOAM 10's `multiComponentMixture` implementation, the solver looks for individual species sub-dictionaries (like `CH4 { ... }`, `O2 { ... }`, etc.) within `physicalProperties`, rather than a single `species` list.

## Root Cause

In OpenFOAM 10, when using `multiComponentMixture` with a chemistry reader, the thermophysical properties file needs to contain individual entry dictionaries for each species, specifying their thermodynamic coefficients. Simply having a `species` list is not sufficient — the solver needs to look up each species by name as a top-level sub-dictionary.

## Proposed Solution

Modify `constant/physicalProperties` to include individual species sub-dictionaries. Based on the existing `thermo.compressibleGas` file structure, add entries like:

```
species
(
    CH4
    O2
    N2
    H2O
    CO2
);

CH4
{
    type        specie;
    model       idealGas;
    mw          16.043;
    sigma       0.3743;
    epsilon     0.0;
    Cp0         35.06;
    omega       0.023;
}

O2
{
    type        specie;
    model       idealGas;
    mw          31.999;
    sigma       0.3164;
    epsilon     0.0;
    Cp0         29.359;
    omega       0.023;
}

N2
{
    type        specie;
    model       idealGas;
    mw          28.014;
    sigma       0.2969;
    epsilon     0.0;
    Cp0         29.124;
    omega       0.023;
}

H2O
{
    type        specie;
    model       idealGas;
    mw          18.015;
    sigma       0.3669;
    epsilon     0.0;
    Cp0         33.599;
    omega       0.023;
}

CO2
{
    type        specie;
    model       idealGas;
    mw          44.01;
    sigma       0.2808;
    epsilon     0.0;
    Cp0         37.135;
    omega       0.023;
}
```

These values are standard thermodynamic properties for each species. They can be found in the OpenFOAM installation under `$FOAM_TUTORIALS/combustion/reactingFoam/laminarFlame/thermophysicalData/` or derived from the existing `thermo.compressibleGas` file. The key point is that each species must have its own named sub-dictionary with at minimum a `type specie;` and `model idealGas;` entry, plus the appropriate transport and thermodynamic coefficients.

After adding these species sub-dictionaries, the chemistry reader will be able to resolve the `CH4` keyword (and the others) when initializing the multi-component mixture.</reviewer_analysis>

# Error Diagnosis and Resolution

## Error Analysis

The error message is clear:

```
Unknown mixture type reactingMixture
Supported mixture types:
4
(
pureMixture
coefficientWilkeMultiComponentMixture
multiComponentMixture
valueMultiComponentMixture
)
```

This indicates that in **OpenFOAM 10** (as seen from the path `/global/cfs/cdirs/m4756/theseus/OpenFOAM-10/`), the mixture type `reactingMixture` is **no longer supported**. The Foundation version (OpenFOAM 10) restructured how reacting mixtures work — the old `reactingMixture` type has been replaced.

## Root Cause

In OpenFOAM 10 (Foundation version), the thermophysical properties setup changed significantly compared to earlier versions. The `reactingMixture` type was removed/replaced, and the new approach uses `multiComponentMixture` combined with a `chemistryReader` to handle species and their thermodynamic data.

Looking at the current `physicalProperties` file, it explicitly sets:

```
mixture         reactingMixture;
```

But in OpenFOAM 10, this needs to be changed to `multiComponentMixture`.

Additionally, the `thermo.compressibleGas` file also references `reactingMixture`, which will cause the same error after fixing the primary one.

## Proposed Solutions

### 1. Fix `constant/physicalProperties`

Change the `mixture` entry from `reactingMixture` to `multiComponentMixture`:

```
thermoType
{
    type            hePsiThermo;
    mixture         multiComponentMixture;
    transport       sutherland;
    thermo          janaf;
    energy          sensibleEnthalpy;
    equationOfState perfectGas;
    specie          specie;
}
```

Also remove the following lines from `physicalProperties` since they belong in the thermo file (`thermo.compressibleGas`) instead:

- Remove `chemistryReader foamChemistryReader;` — this should remain but verify it's still needed.
- Remove `foamChemistryFile "<constant>/reactions";`
- Remove `foamChemistryThermoFile "<constant>/thermo.compressibleGas";`

These last two lines may need to be moved/added to `constant/thermo.compressibleGas` instead.

### 2. Fix `constant/thermo.compressibleGas`

Ensure the `thermoType` in `thermo.compressibleGas` uses `multiComponentMixture` instead of `reactingMixture`:

```
thermoType
{
    type            hePsiThermo;
    mixture         multiComponentMixture;
    transport       sutherland;
    thermo          janaf;
    energy          sensibleEnthalpy;
    equationOfState perfectGas;
    specie          specie;
}
```

Remove the duplicate `chemistryReader`, `foamChemistryFile`, and `foamChemistryThermoFile` entries from this file if they were added there — these should come from `physicalProperties`. Alternatively, keep them in `thermo.compressibleGas` since `multiComponentMixture` requires them to read species data.

### 3. Verify `constant/chemicalProperties`

No changes needed here — the chemistry configuration remains valid.

## Summary

The core issue is that OpenFOAM 10 (Foundation version) no longer supports `reactingMixture` as a mixture type. It has been replaced by `multiComponentMixture`. All thermophysical property files must be updated to use the new mixture type, and the chemistry reader references must point to the correct files so the solver can parse the species and reaction information.

The error message is:

```
keyword species is undefined in dictionary "constant/physicalProperties"
```

This means the `physicalProperties` file does not contain a `species` entry. In OpenFOAM 10 (Foundation version), when using `multiComponentMixture` with a chemistry reader, the `physicalProperties` file needs to include a `species` sub-dictionary or list so that the chemistry framework can identify the available species.

However, looking more carefully at the current `physicalProperties` file, I notice it already has `chemistryReader`, `foamChemistryFile`, and `foamChemistryThermoFile` entries pointing to the `reactions` and `thermo.compressibleGas` files. These files should contain the species information.

The actual problem is likely that the `thermo.compressibleGas` file (which is essentially a copy of `physicalProperties` with minor formatting differences) doesn't contain a top-level `species` keyword either. In OpenFOAM 10's `multiComponentMixture` setup, the species list must be accessible either in `physicalProperties` or through the thermo file.

## Root Cause

In OpenFOAM 10 with `multiComponentMixture`, the species list is required in the thermophysical properties dictionary. Since the `reactions` file contains the species list but `physicalProperties` doesn't, the solver cannot find the `species` keyword.

## Proposed Solution

Add the `species` entry directly to `constant/physicalProperties`. The species list should match what's defined in the `reactions` file:

In `constant/physicalProperties`, add the following entry inside the main dictionary (but outside the `thermoType` block):

```
species

    N2
    H2O

);
```

This is exactly the same list that appears in the `reactions` file, and having it in `physicalProperties` ensures that when the chemistry reader parses the file and loads the reaction mechanism, the species can be resolved correctly.

Alternatively, if the `chemistryReader` mechanism in OpenFOAM 10 expects the species to be defined in `physicalProperties` rather than being read from the `reactions` file directly, this addition should resolve the lookup failure.

## Summary

<review_analysis># Error Diagnosis and Resolution

## Error Analysis

The error message is:

```
keyword specie is undefined in dictionary "constant/physicalProperties/CH4"
```

This means the solver is trying to look up a `specie` sub-dictionary inside the `CH4` file itself (located at `constant/physicalProperties/CH4`), but it can't find one. 

Looking at the current file structure, the `physicalProperties` file contains `species` and then individual species sub-dictionaries (`CH4 { ... }`, `O2 { ... }`, etc.). But the `thermo.compressibleGas` file is essentially a copy of `physicalProperties` with the same content. The error suggests that the chemistry reader is looking for a `specie` entry inside each species file (like `constant/physicalProperties/CH4`), which implies that in OpenFOAM 10, the species thermodynamic data should be defined **directly inside** the `physicalProperties` file as sub-dictionaries, and the separate files in `constant/` (CH4, O2, H2O, N2, CO2) are not being picked up as species definitions.

Wait — re-reading the error more carefully: it says `constant/physicalProperties/CH4`. That file path exists and is indeed the `0/CH4` boundary condition file. The solver is incorrectly trying to open the boundary condition file as a thermophysical properties sub-dictionary.

## Root Cause

The real issue is that the `thermo.compressibleGas` file references `<constant>/thermo.compressibleGas` as the thermo file, but the `physicalProperties` file already contains the species sub-dictionaries. However, the chemistry reader in OpenFOAM 10 with `multiComponentMixture` expects the species thermodynamic data to be in `physicalProperties` directly, and the separate species files in `constant/` (the boundary condition files) are not being used for this purpose.

The problem from Attempt 3 is that the species sub-dictionaries ARE in `physicalProperties` now, yet the error still occurs. This suggests the issue is actually with the `thermo.compressibleGas` file. Looking at it, it's a copy of `physicalProperties` but it lacks the `species` list and species sub-dictionaries. When the chemistry reader tries to load the thermo file, it needs those entries.

## Proposed Solution

The `thermo.compressibleGas` file needs to contain the `species` list and all the species sub-dictionaries (`CH4 { ... }`, `O2 { ... }`, etc.) that were previously only in `physicalProperties`. Add the following to `constant/thermo.compressibleGas`:

1. Add the `species` list inside the main dictionary.
2. Add each species sub-dictionary (`CH4 { ... }`, `O2 { ... }`, `N2 { ... }`, `H2O { ... }`, `CO2 { ... }`) with their thermodynamic properties.

Essentially, `thermo.compressibleGas` should be a complete thermophysical properties file containing both the `thermoType` block AND the species data. The `physicalProperties` file can remain as-is (with `multiComponentMixture` mixture type and species sub-dictionaries), but `thermo.compressibleGas` must also have the species entries for the chemistry reader to successfully parse the reaction mechanism.

Alternatively, a simpler approach: remove the `chemistryReader`, `foamChemistryFile`, and `foamChemistryThermoFile` entries from `physicalProperties` entirely, and put them directly into `thermo.compressibleGas`. Then ensure `thermo.compressibleGas` has the `species` list and all species sub-dictionaries. The `physicalProperties` file can stay with just the `thermoType` and species/sub-dictionaries (it already works as a standalone file).</review_analysis>

<reviewer_analysis># Error Diagnosis and Resolution

## Error Analysis

The error message is:

```
keyword specie is undefined in dictionary "constant/physicalProperties/CH4"
```

This means the solver is trying to look up a `specie` sub-dictionary inside the `CH4` file itself (located at `constant/physicalProperties/CH4`), but it can't find one. 

Looking at the current file structure, the `physicalProperties` file contains `species` and then individual species sub-dictionaries (`CH4 { ... }`, `O2 { ... }`, etc.). But the `thermo.compressibleGas` file is essentially a copy of `physicalProperties` with the same content. The error suggests that the chemistry reader is looking for a `specie` entry inside each species file (like `constant/physicalProperties/CH4`), which implies that in OpenFOAM 10, the species thermodynamic data should be defined **directly inside** the `physicalProperties` file as sub-dictionaries, and the separate files in `constant/` (CH4, O2, H2O, N2, CO2) are not being picked up as species definitions.

Wait — re-reading the error more carefully: it says `constant/physicalProperties/CH4`. That file path exists and is indeed the `0/CH4` boundary condition file. The solver is incorrectly trying to open the boundary condition file as a thermophysical properties sub-dictionary.

## Root Cause

The real issue is that the `thermo.compressibleGas` file references `<constant>/thermo.compressibleGas` as the thermo file, but the `physicalProperties` file already contains the species sub-dictionaries. However, the chemistry reader in OpenFOAM 10 with `multiComponentMixture` expects the species thermodynamic data to be in `physicalProperties` directly, and the separate species files in `constant/` (the boundary condition files) are not being used for this purpose.

The problem from Attempt 3 is that the species sub-dictionaries ARE in `physicalProperties` now, yet the error still occurs. This suggests the issue is actually with the `thermo.compressibleGas` file. Looking at it, it's a copy of `physicalProperties` but it lacks the `species` list and species sub-dictionaries. When the chemistry reader tries to load the thermo file, it needs those entries.

## Proposed Solution

The `thermo.compressibleGas` file needs to contain the `species` list and all the species sub-dictionaries (`CH4 { ... }`, `O2 { ... }`, etc.) that were previously only in `physicalProperties`. Add the following to `constant/thermo.compressibleGas`:

1. Add the `species` list inside the main dictionary.
2. Add each species sub-dictionary (`CH4 { ... }`, `O2 { ... }`, `N2 { ... }`, `H2O { ... }`, `CO2 { ... }`) with their thermodynamic properties.

Essentially, `thermo.compressibleGas` should be a complete thermophysical properties file containing both the `thermoType` block AND the species data. The `physicalProperties` file can remain as-is (with `multiComponentMixture` mixture type and species sub-dictionaries), but `thermo.compressibleGas` must also have the species entries for the chemistry reader to successfully parse the reaction mechanism.

Alternatively, a simpler approach: remove the `chemistryReader`, `foamChemistryFile`, and `foamChemistryThermoFile` entries from `physicalProperties` entirely, and put them directly into `thermo.compressibleGas`. Then ensure `thermo.compressibleGas` has the `species` list and all species sub-dictionaries. The `physicalProperties` file can stay with just the `thermoType` and species/sub-dictionaries (it already works as a standalone file).</reviewer_analysis>

# Error Diagnosis and Resolution

## Error Analysis

The error message is clear:

```
Unknown mixture type reactingMixture
Supported mixture types:
4
(
pureMixture
coefficientWilkeMultiComponentMixture
multiComponentMixture
valueMultiComponentMixture
)
```

This indicates that in **OpenFOAM 10** (as seen from the path `/global/cfs/cdirs/m4756/theseus/OpenFOAM-10/`), the mixture type `reactingMixture` is **no longer supported**. The Foundation version (OpenFOAM 10) restructured how reacting mixtures work — the old `reactingMixture` type has been replaced.

## Root Cause

In OpenFOAM 10 (Foundation version), the thermophysical properties setup changed significantly compared to earlier versions. The `reactingMixture` type was removed/replaced, and the new approach uses `multiComponentMixture` combined with a `chemistryReader` to handle species and their thermodynamic data.

Looking at the current `physicalProperties` file, it explicitly sets:

```
mixture         reactingMixture;
```

But in OpenFOAM 10, this needs to be changed to `multiComponentMixture`.

Additionally, the `thermo.compressibleGas` file also references `reactingMixture`, which will cause the same error after fixing the primary one.

## Proposed Solutions

### 1. Fix `constant/physicalProperties`

Change the `mixture` entry from `reactingMixture` to `multiComponentMixture`:

```
thermoType
{
    type            hePsiThermo;
    mixture         multiComponentMixture;
    transport       sutherland;
    thermo          janaf;
    energy          sensibleEnthalpy;
    equationOfState perfectGas;
    specie          specie;
}
```

Also remove the following lines from `physicalProperties` since they belong in the thermo file (`thermo.compressibleGas`) instead:

- Remove `chemistryReader foamChemistryReader;` — this should remain but verify it's still needed.
- Remove `foamChemistryFile "<constant>/reactions";`
- Remove `foamChemistryThermoFile "<constant>/thermo.compressibleGas";`

These last two lines may need to be moved/added to `constant/thermo.compressibleGas` instead.

### 2. Fix `constant/thermo.compressibleGas`

Ensure the `thermoType` in `thermo.compressibleGas` uses `multiComponentMixture` instead of `reactingMixture`:

```
thermoType
{
    type            hePsiThermo;
    mixture         multiComponentMixture;
    transport       sutherland;
    thermo          janaf;
    energy          sensibleEnthalpy;
    equationOfState perfectGas;
    specie          specie;
}
```

Remove the duplicate `chemistryReader`, `foamChemistryFile`, and `foamChemistryThermoFile` entries from this file if they were added there — these should come from `physicalProperties`. Alternatively, keep them in `thermo.compressibleGas` since `multiComponentMixture` requires them to read species data.

### 3. Verify `constant/chemicalProperties`

No changes needed here — the chemistry configuration remains valid.

## Summary

The core issue is that OpenFOAM 10 (Foundation version) no longer supports `reactingMixture` as a mixture type. It has been replaced by `multiComponentMixture`. All thermophysical property files must be updated to use the new mixture type, and the chemistry reader references must point to the correct files so the solver can parse the species and reaction information.

The root problem is that the `physicalProperties` file has `chemistryReader foamChemistryReader;`, `foamChemistryFile "<constant>/reactions";`, and `foamChemistryThermoFile "<constant>/thermo.compressibleGas";`. In OpenFOAM 10, the chemistry reader reads the reactions file to get the species list, and then tries to find thermophysical data for each species. But because the species thermodynamic data is already embedded in `physical.properties` as sub-dictionaries, and the thermo file (`thermo.compressibleGas`) is essentially a duplicate, the reader gets confused and falls back to treating species names as file paths.

## Root Cause

In OpenFOAM 10, when using `multiComponentMixture` with `chemistryReader`, the chemistry framework expects to read species thermodynamic data from the thermo file. The current `thermo.compressibleGas` file is a copy of `physicalProperties` and contains the species sub-dictionaries, but the chemistry reader is still failing to initialize properly — likely because the `thermoType` block in `thermo.compressibleGas` conflicts or the file isn't being parsed correctly as a thermo file.

## Proposed Solution

The cleanest approach is to **separate concerns**:

1. **`constant/physicalProperties`**: Keep it simple with just the `thermoType` using `multiComponentMixture`, and remove the `chemistryReader`, `foamChemistryFile`, and `foamChemistryThermoFile` entries. No species list or species sub-dictionaries needed here.

2. **`constant/thermo.compressibleGas`**: Make this the dedicated thermophysical properties file. It should contain:
   - The `thermoType` block with `mixture multiComponentMixture`
   - The `species` list
   - Individual species sub-dictionaries (`CH4 { ... }`, `O2 { ... }`, `N2 { ... }`, `H2O { ... }`, `CO2 { ... }`)
   - Add `chemistryReader foamChemistryReader;` 
   - Add `foamChemistryFile "<constant>/reactions";`
   - Add `foamChemistryThermoFile "<constant>/thermo.compressibleGas";` (self-reference is fine)

This way, when the chemistry reader processes the reactions file and sees the species list, it will find the corresponding thermophysical data in `thermo.compressibleGas` where it belongs. The `physicalProperties` file serves as the base thermo file for the mixture without involving the chemistry reader.

3. **Verify `constant/reactions`**: Ensure it has the `species` list and the `reactions` sub-dictionary — this should remain unchanged.

The key change is splitting the responsibilities: `physicalProperties` becomes a pure thermo file for the mixture, and `thermo.compressibleGas` becomes the full thermo+chemistry file that the reader actually consumes.

<review_analysis># Error Diagnosis and Resolution

## Error Analysis

The error message is:

```
keyword specie is undefined in dictionary "constant/physicalProperties/CH4"
```

This means the solver is looking for a `specie` sub-dictionary **inside** the file `constant/physicalProperties/CH4`. Looking at the file listing, `constant/physicalProperties/CH4` is actually the **boundary condition file** from `0/CH4`, not a thermophysical properties sub-dictionary.

This confirms my suspicion from Attempt 4: the chemistry reader in OpenFOAM 10 with `multiComponentMixture` is incorrectly picking up the boundary condition files (from the `0/` directory) as thermophysical property files. The solver is walking through the species list (`CH4`, `O2`, `N2`, `H2O`, `CO2`) and for each species name, it's trying to find a corresponding file in `constant/` — and it finds the boundary condition file `0/CH4` instead of a proper thermophysical properties sub-dictionary.

The root problem is that the `physicalProperties` file has `chemistryReader foamChemistryReader;`, `foamChemistryFile "<constant>/reactions";`, and `foamChemistryThermoFile "<constant>/thermo.compressibleGas";`. In OpenFOAM 10, the chemistry reader reads the reactions file to get the species list, and then tries to find thermophysical data for each species. But because the species thermodynamic data is already embedded in `physical.properties` as sub-dictionaries, and the thermo file (`thermo.compressibleGas`) is essentially a duplicate, the reader gets confused and falls back to treating species names as file paths.

## Root Cause

In OpenFOAM 10, when using `multiComponentMixture` with `chemistryReader`, the chemistry framework expects to read species thermodynamic data from the thermo file. The current `thermo.compressibleGas` file is a copy of `physicalProperties` and contains the species sub-dictionaries, but the chemistry reader is still failing to initialize properly — likely because the `thermoType` block in `thermo.compressibleGas` conflicts or the file isn't being parsed correctly as a thermo file.

## Proposed Solution

The cleanest approach is to **separate concerns**:

1. **`constant/physicalProperties`**: Keep it simple with just the `thermoType` using `multiComponentMixture`, and remove the `chemistryReader`, `foamChemistryFile`, and `foamChemistryThermoFile` entries. No species list or species sub-dictionaries needed here.

2. **`constant/thermo.compressibleGas`**: Make this the dedicated thermophysical properties file. It should contain:
   - The `thermoType` block with `mixture multiComponentMixture`
   - The `species` list
   - Individual species sub-dictionaries (`CH4 { ... }`, `O2 { ... }`, `N2 { ... }`, `H2O { ... }`, `CO2 { ... }`)
   - Add `chemistryReader foamChemistryReader;` 
   - Add `foamChemistryFile "<constant>/reactions";`
   - Add `foamChemistryThermoFile "<constant>/thermo.compressibleGas";` (self-reference is fine)

This way, when the chemistry reader processes the reactions file and sees the species list, it will find the corresponding thermophysical data in `thermo.compressibleGas` where it belongs. The `physicalProperties` file serves as the base thermo file for the mixture without involving the chemistry reader.

3. **Verify `constant/reactions`**: Ensure it has the `species` list and the `reactions` sub-dictionary — this should remain unchanged.

The key change is splitting the responsibilities: `physicalProperties` becomes a pure thermo file for the mixture, and `thermo.compressibleGas` becomes the full thermo+chemistry file that the reader actually consumes.</review_analysis>

<reviewer_analysis># Error Diagnosis and Resolution

## Error Analysis

The error message is:

```
keyword specie is undefined in dictionary "constant/physicalProperties/CH4"
```

This means the solver is looking for a `specie` sub-dictionary **inside** the file `constant/physicalProperties/CH4`. Looking at the file listing, `constant/physicalProperties/CH4` is actually the **boundary condition file** from `0/CH4`, not a thermophysical properties sub-dictionary.

This confirms my suspicion from Attempt 4: the chemistry reader in OpenFOAM 10 with `multiComponentMixture` is incorrectly picking up the boundary condition files (from the `0/` directory) as thermophysical property files. The solver is walking through the species list (`CH4`, `O2`, `N2`, `H2O`, `CO2`) and for each species name, it's trying to find a corresponding file in `constant/` — and it finds the boundary condition file `0/CH4` instead of a proper thermophysical properties sub-dictionary.

The root problem is that the `physicalProperties` file has `chemistryReader foamChemistryReader;`, `foamChemistryFile "<constant>/reactions";`, and `foamChemistryThermoFile "<constant>/thermo.compressibleGas";`. In OpenFOAM 10, the chemistry reader reads the reactions file to get the species list, and then tries to find thermophysical data for each species. But because the species thermodynamic data is already embedded in `physical.properties` as sub-dictionaries, and the thermo file (`thermo.compressibleGas`) is essentially a duplicate, the reader gets confused and falls back to treating species names as file paths.

## Root Cause

In OpenFOAM 10, when using `multiComponentMixture` with `chemistryReader`, the chemistry framework expects to read species thermodynamic data from the thermo file. The current `thermo.compressibleGas` file is a copy of `physicalProperties` and contains the species sub-dictionaries, but the chemistry reader is still failing to initialize properly — likely because the `thermoType` block in `thermo.compressibleGas` conflicts or the file isn't being parsed correctly as a thermo file.

## Proposed Solution

The cleanest approach is to **separate concerns**:

1. **`constant/physicalProperties`**: Keep it simple with just the `thermoType` using `multiComponentMixture`, and remove the `chemistryReader`, `foamChemistryFile`, and `foamChemistryThermoFile` entries. No species list or species sub-dictionaries needed here.

2. **`constant/thermo.compressibleGas`**: Make this the dedicated thermophysical properties file. It should contain:
   - The `thermoType` block with `mixture multiComponentMixture`
   - The `species` list
   - Individual species sub-dictionaries (`CH4 { ... }`, `O2 { ... }`, `N2 { ... }`, `H2O { ... }`, `CO2 { ... }`)
   - Add `chemistryReader foamChemistryReader;` 
   - Add `foamChemistryFile "<constant>/reactions";`
   - Add `foamChemistryThermoFile "<constant>/thermo.compressibleGas";` (self-reference is fine)

This way, when the chemistry reader processes the reactions file and sees the species list, it will find the corresponding thermophysical data in `thermo.compressibleGas` where it belongs. The `physicalProperties` file serves as the base thermo file for the mixture without involving the chemistry reader.

3. **Verify `constant/reactions`**: Ensure it has the `species` list and the `reactions` sub-dictionary — this should remain unchanged.

The key change is splitting the responsibilities: `physicalProperties` becomes a pure thermo file for the mixture, and `thermo.compressibleGas` becomes the full thermo+chemistry file that the reader actually consumes.</reviewer_analysis>

# Error Diagnosis and Resolution

## Error Analysis

The error message is clear:

```
Unknown mixture type reactingMixture
Supported mixture types:
4
(
pureMixture
coefficientWilkeMultiComponentMixture
multiComponentMixture
valueMultiComponentMixture
)
```

This indicates that in **OpenFOAM 10** (as seen from the path `/global/cfs/cdirs/m4756/theseus/OpenFOAM-10/`), the mixture type `reactingMixture` is **no longer supported**. The Foundation version (OpenFOAM 10) restructured how reacting mixtures work — the old `reactingMixture` type has been replaced.

## Root Cause

In OpenFOAM 10 (Foundation version), the thermophysical properties setup changed significantly compared to earlier versions. The `reactingMixture` type was removed/replaced, and the new approach uses `multiComponentMixture` combined with a `chemistryReader` to handle species and their thermodynamic data.

Looking at the current `physicalProperties` file, it explicitly sets:

```
mixture         reactingMixture;
```

But in OpenFOAM 10, this needs to be changed to `multiComponentMixture`.

Additionally, the `thermo.compressibleGas` file also references `reactingMixture`, which will cause the same error after fixing the primary one.

## Proposed Solutions

### 1. Fix `constant/physicalProperties`

Change the `mixture` entry from `reactingMixture` to `multiComponentMixture`:

```
thermoType
{
    type            hePsiThermo;
    mixture         multiComponentMixture;
    transport       sutherland;
    thermo          janaf;
    energy          sensibleEnthalpy;
    equationOfState perfectGas;
    specie          specie;
}
```

Also remove the following lines from `physicalProperties` since they belong in the thermo file (`thermo.compressibleGas`) instead:

- Remove `chemistryReader foamChemistryReader;` — this should remain but verify it's still needed.
- Remove `foamChemistryFile "<constant>/reactions";`
- Remove `foamChemistryThermoFile "<constant>/thermo.compressibleGas";`

These last two lines may need to be moved/added to `constant/thermo.compressibleGas` instead.

### 2. Fix `constant/thermo.compressibleGas`

Ensure the `thermoType` in `thermo.compressibleGas` uses `multiComponentMixture` instead of `reactingMixture`:

```
thermoType
{
    type            hePsiThermo;
    mixture         multiComponentMixture;
    transport       sutherland;
    thermo          janaf;
    energy          sensibleEnthalpy;
    equationOfState perfectGas;
    specie          specie;
}
```

Remove the duplicate `chemistryReader`, `foamChemistryFile`, and `foamChemistryThermoFile` entries from this file if they were added there — these should come from `physicalProperties`. Alternatively, keep them in `thermo.compressibleGas` since `multiComponentMixture` requires them to read species data.

### 3. Verify `constant/chemicalProperties`

No changes needed here — the chemistry configuration remains valid.

## Summary

The core issue is that OpenFOAM 10 (Foundation version) no longer supports `reactingMixture` as a mixture type. It has been replaced by `multiComponentMixture`. All thermophysical property files must be updated to use the new mixture type, and the chemistry reader references must point to the correct files so the solver can parse the species and reaction information.

# Error Diagnosis and Resolution

## Error Analysis

The error message is:

```
keyword species is undefined in dictionary "constant/physicalProperties"
```

This means the `physicalProperties` file does not contain a `species` entry. In OpenFOAM 10 (Foundation version), when using `multiComponentMixture` with a chemistry reader, the `physicalProperties` file needs to include a `species` sub-dictionary or list so that the chemistry framework can identify the available species.

However, looking more carefully at the current `physicalProperties` file, I notice it already has `chemistryReader`, `foamChemistryFile`, and `foamChemistryThermoFile` entries pointing to the `reactions` and `thermo.compressibleGas` files. These files should contain the species information.

The actual problem is likely that the `thermo.compressibleGas` file (which is essentially a copy of `physicalProperties` with minor formatting differences) doesn't contain a top-level `species` keyword either. In OpenFOAM 10's `multiComponentMixture` setup, the species list must be accessible either in `physicalProperties` or through the thermo file.

## Root Cause

In OpenFOAM 10 with `multiComponentMixture`, the species list is required in the thermophysical properties dictionary. Since the `reactions` file contains the species list but `physicalProperties` doesn't, the solver cannot find the `species` keyword.

## Proposed Solution

Add the `species` entry directly to `constant/physicalProperties`. The species list should match what's defined in the `reactions` file:

In `constant/physicalProperties`, add the following entry inside the main dictionary (but outside the `thermoType` block):

```

<Review_Analysis>
# Error Diagnosis and Resolution

## Error Analysis

The error message is:

```
keyword specie is undefined in dictionary "constant/physicalProperties/CH4"
```

This means the solver is looking for a `specie` sub-dictionary **inside** the file `constant/physicalProperties/CH4`. Looking at the file listing, `constant/physicalProperties/CH4` is actually the **boundary condition file** from `0/CH4`, not a thermophysical properties sub-dictionary.

This confirms my suspicion from Attempt 4: the chemistry reader in OpenFOAM 10 with `multiComponentMixture` is incorrectly picking up the boundary condition files (from the `0/` directory) as thermophysical property files. The solver is walking through the species list (`CH4`, `O2`, `N2`, `H2O`, `CO2`) and for each species name, it's trying to find a corresponding file in `constant/` — and it finds the boundary condition file `0/CH4` instead of a proper thermophysical properties sub-dictionary.

The root problem is that the `physicalProperties` file has `chemistryReader foamChemistryReader;`, `foamChemistryFile "<constant>/reactions";`, and `foamChemistryThermoFile "<constant>/thermo.compressibleGas";`. In OpenFOAM 10, the chemistry reader reads the reactions file to get the species list, and then tries to find thermophysical data for each species. But because the species thermodynamic data is already embedded in `physical.properties` as sub-dictionaries, and the thermo file (`thermo.compressibleGas`) is essentially a duplicate, the reader gets confused and falls back to treating species names as file paths.

## Root Cause

In OpenFOAM 10, when using `multiComponentMixture` with `chemistryReader`, the chemistry framework expects to read species thermodynamic data from the thermo file. The current `thermo.compressibleGas` file is a copy of `physicalProperties` and contains the species sub-dictionaries, but the chemistry reader is still failing to initialize properly — likely because the `thermoType` block in `thermo.compressibleGas` conflicts or the file isn't being parsed correctly as a thermo file.

## Proposed Solution

The cleanest approach is to **separate concerns**:

1. **`constant/physicalProperties`**: Keep it simple with just the `thermoType` using `multiComponentMixture`, and remove the `chemistryReader`, `foamChemistryFile`, and `foamChemistryThermoFile` entries. No species list or species sub-dictionaries needed here.

2. **`constant/thermo.compressibleGas`**: Make this the dedicated thermophysical properties file. It should contain:
   - The `thermoType` block with `mixture multiComponentMixture`
   - The `species` list
   - Individual species sub-dictionaries (`CH4 { ... }`, `O2 { ... }`, `N2 { ... }`, `H2O { ... }`, `CO2 { ... }`)
   - Add `chemistryReader foamChemistryReader;` 
   - Add `foamChemistryFile "<constant>/reactions";`
   - Add `foamChemistryThermoFile "<constant>/thermo.compressibleGas";` (self-reference is fine)

This way, when the chemistry reader processes the reactions file and sees the species list, it will find the corresponding thermophysical data in `thermo.compressibleGas` where it belongs. The `physicalProperties` file serves as the base thermo file for the mixture without involving the chemistry reader.

3. **Verify `constant/reactions`**: Ensure it has the `species` list and the `reactions` sub-dictionary — this should remain unchanged.

The key change is splitting the responsibilities: `physicalProperties` becomes a pure thermo file for the mixture, and `thermo.compressibleGas` becomes the full thermo+chemistry file that the reader actually consumes.
</Review_Analysis>

<review_analysis># Error Diagnosis and Resolution

## Error Analysis

The error message is:

```
keyword species is undefined in dictionary "constant/physicalProperties"
```

This is the same error as Attempt 2, meaning the `species` list was added to `physicalProperties` but the solver still cannot find it. The error originates from `physicalProperties` at lines 12-21, which corresponds to the `thermoType` block area.

Looking at the current `physicalProperties` file carefully:

```
thermoType
{
    type            hePsiThermo;
    mixture         multiComponentMixture;
    transport       sutherland;
    thermo          janaf;
    energy          sensibleEnthalpy;
    equationOfState perfectGas;
    specie          specie;
}
```

The `specie` keyword is present, but the solver is looking for `species` (plural). In OpenFOAM 10 with `multiComponentMixture`, the solver expects a top-level `species` list in the thermophysical properties dictionary. Having `specie` (singular) is not sufficient — the solver needs the `species` keyword to iterate over the species.

Additionally, the `thermo.compressibleGas` file has identical content to `physicalProperties`, which creates confusion. The chemistry reader is likely reading `physicalProperties` first and failing to find the `species` keyword.

## Root Cause

In OpenFOAM 10's `multiComponentMixture` implementation, the thermophysical properties dictionary must contain a top-level `species` entry (a list of species names) for the chemistry framework to resolve species thermodynamic data. The current `physicalProperties` file has `specie` (singular, for the mixture specifier) but is missing the `species` (plural, list of component names).

## Proposed Solution

**Modify `constant/physicalProperties`:**

1. **Remove** the `specie` keyword from the `thermoType` block (it's not needed for `multiComponentMixture`).

2. **Add** a `species` list inside the main dictionary (at the same level as `thermoType`). Place it right after the closing brace of `thermoType`:

```
species
(
    CH4
    O2
    N2
    H2O
    CO2
);
```

So the final `physicalProperties` should look like:

```
thermoType
{
    type            hePsiThermo;
    mixture         multiComponentMixture;
    transport       sutherland;
    thermo          janaf;
    energy          sensibleEnthalpy;
    equationOfState perfectGas;
}

species
(
    CH4
    O2
    N2
    H2O
    CO2
);

inertSpecie N2;
```

3. **Keep `thermo.compressibleGas`** as a near-identical copy of `physicalProperties` (it can remain the same content since it's not causing harm there).

4. **Ensure `constant/reactions`** retains its `species` list and `reactions` sub-dictionary — this should remain unchanged.

The critical fix is adding the `species` (plural) list to `physicalProperties`. The `specie` keyword alone is not enough for OpenFOAM 10's `multiComponentMixture` — the chemistry reader needs the `species` list to know which species to look up and how to index their thermodynamic data.</review_analysis>

<reviewer_analysis># Error Diagnosis and Resolution

## Error Analysis

The error message is:

```
keyword species is undefined in dictionary "constant/physicalProperties"
```

This is the same error as Attempt 2, meaning the `species` list was added to `physicalProperties` but the solver still cannot find it. The error originates from `physicalProperties` at lines 12-21, which corresponds to the `thermoType` block area.

Looking at the current `physicalProperties` file carefully:

```
thermoType
{
    type            hePsiThermo;
    mixture         multiComponentMixture;
    transport       sutherland;
    thermo          janaf;
    energy          sensibleEnthalpy;
    equationOfState perfectGas;
    specie          specie;
}
```

The `specie` keyword is present, but the solver is looking for `species` (plural). In OpenFOAM 10 with `multiComponentMixture`, the solver expects a top-level `species` list in the thermophysical properties dictionary. Having `specie` (singular) is not sufficient — the solver needs the `species` keyword to iterate over the species.

Additionally, the `thermo.compressibleGas` file has identical content to `physicalProperties`, which creates confusion. The chemistry reader is likely reading `physicalProperties` first and failing to find the `species` keyword.

## Root Cause

In OpenFOAM 10's `multiComponentMixture` implementation, the thermophysical properties dictionary must contain a top-level `species` entry (a list of species names) for the chemistry framework to resolve species thermodynamic data. The current `physicalProperties` file has `specie` (singular, for the mixture specifier) but is missing the `species` (plural, list of component names).

## Proposed Solution

**Modify `constant/physicalProperties`:**

1. **Remove** the `specie` keyword from the `thermoType` block (it's not needed for `multiComponentMixture`).

2. **Add** a `species` list inside the main dictionary (at the same level as `thermoType`). Place it right after the closing brace of `thermoType`:

```
species
(
    CH4
    O2
    N2
    H2O
    CO2
);
```

So the final `physicalProperties` should look like:

```
thermoType
{
    type            hePsiThermo;
    mixture         multiComponentMixture;
    transport       sutherland;
    thermo          janaf;
    energy          sensibleEnthalpy;
    equationOfState perfectGas;
}

species
(
    CH4
    O2
    N2
    H2O
    CO2
);

inertSpecie N2;
```

3. **Keep `thermo.compressibleGas`** as a near-identical copy of `physicalProperties` (it can remain the same content since it's not causing harm there).

4. **Ensure `constant/reactions`** retains its `species` list and `reactions` sub-dictionary — this should remain unchanged.

The critical fix is adding the `species` (plural) list to `physicalProperties`. The `specie` keyword alone is not enough for OpenFOAM 10's `multiComponentMixture` — the chemistry reader needs the `species` list to know which species to look up and how to index their thermodynamic data.</reviewer_analysis>

# Error Diagnosis and Resolution

## Error Analysis

The error message is clear:

```
Unknown mixture type reactingMixture
Supported mixture types:
4
(
pureMixture
coefficientWilkeMultiComponentMixture
multiComponentMixture
valueMultiComponentMixture
)
```

This indicates that in **OpenFOAM 10** (as seen from the path `/global/cfs/cdirs/m4756/theseus/OpenFOAM-10/`), the mixture type `reactingMixture` is **no longer supported**. The Foundation version (OpenFOAM 10) restructured how reacting mixtures work — the old `reactingMixture` type has been replaced.

## Root Cause

In OpenFOAM 10 (Foundation version), the thermophysical properties setup changed significantly compared to earlier versions. The `reactingMixture` type was removed/replaced, and the new approach uses `multiComponentMixture` combined with a `chemistryReader` to handle species and their thermodynamic data.

Looking at the current `physicalProperties` file, it explicitly sets:

```
mixture         reactingMixture;
```

But in OpenFOAM 10, this needs to be changed to `multiComponentMixture`.

Additionally, the `thermo.compressibleGas` file also references `reactingMixture`, which will cause the same error after fixing the primary one.

## Proposed Solutions

### 1. Fix `constant/physicalProperties`

Change the `mixture` entry from `reactingMixture` to `multiComponentMixture`:

```
thermoType
{
    type            hePsiThermo;
    mixture         multiComponentMixture;
    transport       sutherland;
    thermo          janaf;
    energy          sensibleEnthalpy;
    equationOfState perfectGas;
    specie          specie;
}
```

Also remove the following lines from `physicalProperties` since they belong in the thermo file (`thermo.compressibleGas`) instead:

- Remove `chemistryReader foamChemistryReader;` — this should remain but verify it's still needed.
- Remove `foamChemistryFile "<constant>/reactions";`
- Remove `foamChemistryThermoFile "<constant>/thermo.compressibleGas";`

These last two lines may need to be moved/added to `constant/thermo.compressibleGas` instead.

### 2. Fix `constant/thermo.compressibleGas`

Ensure the `thermoType` in `thermo.compressibleGas` uses `multiComponentMixture` instead of `reactingMixture`:

```
thermoType
{
    type            hePsiThermo;
    mixture         multiComponentMixture;
    transport       sutherland;
    thermo          janaf;
    energy          sensibleEnthalpy;
    equationOfState perfectGas;
    specie          specie;
}
```

Remove the duplicate `chemistryReader`, `foamChemistryFile`, and `foamChemistryThermoFile` entries from this file if they were added there — these should come from `physicalProperties`. Alternatively, keep them in `thermo.compressibleGas` since `multiComponentMixture` requires them to read species data.

### 3. Verify `constant/chemicalProperties`

No changes needed here — the chemistry configuration remains valid.

## Summary

The core issue is that OpenFOAM 10 (Foundation version) no longer supports `reactingMixture` as a mixture type. It has been replaced by `multiComponentMixture`. All thermophysical property files must be updated to use the new mixture type, and the chemistry reader references must point to the correct files so the solver can parse the species and reaction information.
</Review_Analysis>

# Error Diagnosis and Resolution

## Error Analysis

The error message is:

```
keyword CH4 is undefined in dictionary "constant/physicalProperties"
```

This means the `physicalProperties` file does not contain individual species entries for `CH4`, `O2`, `N2`, `H2O`, or `CO2`. In OpenFOAM 10 with `multiComponentMixture`, the solver expects each species to be defined as a separate entry in the `physicalProperties` dictionary (not just a `species` list).

Looking at the history of attempts:
- Attempt 1: Fixed `reactingMixture` → `multiComponentMixture` ✓
- Attempt 2: Added `species` list ✓ but the error changed to "keyword CH4 undefined"
- Now: The error specifically says `CH4` is undefined (singular, not plural)

This tells me that in OpenFOAM 10's `multiComponentMixture` implementation, the solver looks for individual species sub-dictionaries (like `CH4 { ... }`, `O2 { ... }`, etc.) within `physicalProperties`, rather than a single `species` list.

## Root Cause

In OpenFOAM 10, when using `multiComponentMixture` with a chemistry reader, the thermophysical properties file needs to contain individual entry dictionaries for each species, specifying their thermodynamic coefficients. Simply having a `species` list is not sufficient — the solver needs to look up each species by name as a top-level sub-dictionary.

## Proposed Solution

Modify `constant/physicalProperties` to include individual species sub-dictionaries. Based on the existing `thermo.compressibleGas` file structure, add entries like:

```
species
(
    CH4
    O2
    N2
    H2O
    CO2
);

CH4
{

    mw          16.043;

O2

    type        specie;

N2

}

H2O
{
    type        specie;

    sigma       0.3669;
    epsilon     0.0;
    Cp0         33.599;
    omega       0.023;
}

CO2
{
    type        specie;
    model       idealGas;
    mw          44.01;
    sigma       0.2808;
    epsilon     0.0;
    Cp0         37.135;
    omega       0.023;
}
```

These values are standard thermodynamic properties for each species. They can be found in the OpenFOAM installation under `$FOAM_TUTORIALS/combustion/reactingFoam/laminarFlame/thermophysicalData/` or derived from the existing `thermo.compressibleGas` file. The key point is that each species must have its own named sub-dictionary with at minimum a `type specie;` and `model idealGas;` entry, plus the appropriate transport and thermodynamic coefficients.

After adding these species sub-dictionaries, the chemistry reader will be able to resolve the `CH4` keyword (and the others) when initializing the multi-component mixture.
